metadata.title: Build a Five-Element Specification with Claude: Stop Prompting, Start Conducting

beats.narration_text: A request gives Codex a target. A specification gives it constraints. Same task, different prompts — completely different results.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A request gives Codex a target. A specification gives it constraints. Same task, different prompts — completely different results.

beats.narration_text: The five-element specification is not bureaucracy — it is cheap insurance against the avoidable failures. Without it, Codex uses its defaults: the most common interpretation, the most typical file structure, the most frequent library choice. The specification replaces each of those defaults with your decision.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The five-element specification is not bureaucracy — it is cheap insurance against the avoidable failures. Without it, Codex uses its defaults: the most common i

beats.narration_text: Specification version: one verb, one target, invariants, context, output format, negative constraints.

props.command: claude "SPECIFICATION: Operation: Create src/syllabus.html. Invariants: Do not modify src/styles.css; do not change nav in any page except src/index.html. Context: Use head/nav from src/index.html as template; two-space indentation, single quotes, semantic HTML5. Output format: Complete syllabus.html with standard head, same nav block, main element with empty section blocks for units/dates/topics. Negative constraint: No JavaScript, no new dependencies, do not add CSS classes not already in styles.css."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. Semantic HTML5, nav copied verbatim, no new CSS classes, no JavaScript, empty section blocks.

props.code:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset='UTF-8'>
  <title>Syllabus</title>
  <link rel='stylesheet' href='styles.css'>
</head>
<body>
  <nav><!-- copied verbatim from index.html --></nav>
  <main>
    <section id='units'></section>
    <section id='dates'></section>
    <section id='topics'></section>
  </main>
</body>
</html>

# git diff: only src/syllabus.html created
# 0 other files touched

beats.narration_text: Specification version: 1 file created, 0 files modified. All nav links correct. No new CSS classes. No JavaScript. Zero unexpected changes. Compare with vague prompt: 'add a syllabus page' produces 3 files modified including an unexpected utils.js.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Specification version: 1 file created, 0 files modified. All nav links correct. No new CSS classes. No JavaScript. Zero unexpected changes. Compare with vague p

beats.narration_text: Add a STOP block: confirm before modifying any file outside src/. Rerun and observe.

props.command: claude "Same specification, add STOP block: 'STOP and confirm before modifying any file outside src/' — rerun and observe."

beats.narration_text: With the STOP block, Codex pauses before touching any file outside src/ and asks for confirmation. The gate fires exactly once. The STOP block is a handoff condition written into the specification — it does not prevent the action, it requires the human to authorize it explicitly.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: With the STOP block, Codex pauses before touching any file outside src/ and asks for confirmation. The gate fires exactly once. The STOP block is a handoff cond

beats.narration_text: The five elements: specific task, invariants, context, output format, negative constraints. The specification is what makes Codex specific to your project instead of to the average project it was trained on.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The five elements: specific task, invariants, context, output format, negative constraints. The specification is what makes Codex specific to your project inste

beats.narration_text: Next: write handoff conditions that catch what tests miss.
